albums.title: Discovery
albums.reason: With Digital Love, One More Time, and Superheroes all sitting in your all-time top tracks, this is the album you've clearly lived inside — time to revisit it front to back.

albums.title: In Rainbows
albums.reason: Reckoner is your third most-played track of all time, and it lives here — this album deserves a full uninterrupted listen again.

albums.title: The Dark Side of the Moon
albums.reason: Pink Floyd sits at nearly 1,200 all-time plays for you, and this is the cornerstone — probably been a while since you let it play through completely.

albums.title: Melody A.M.
albums.reason: Röyksopp is in your all-time top five with over 1,000 plays, and this debut is where their chilled, cinematic sound was at its most pure and unhurried.

albums.title: Leftism
albums.reason: Release the Pressure is your fourth most-played track ever, and it lives on this album — one of the great British electronic records and clearly a cornerstone of your taste.

albums.title: Moon Safari
albums.reason: Air sits in your all-time top seven with over 1,000 plays — Moon Safari is their most iconic statement and probably overdue for a proper revisit.

albums.title: In Sides
albums.reason: Orbital has 777 all-time plays in your library and this double album is one of their most expansive, immersive works — the kind of deep cut that rewards the headphone treatment.

albums.title: Second Toughest in the Infants
albums.reason: Born Slippy sits in your all-time top 20 tracks and Underworld has 633 plays — this is their most sprawling, atmospheric record and a perfect vault dive.

albums.title: Idealism
albums.reason: If Justice and Daft Punk are two of your most-played artists, Digitalism's debut sits right in that French-filtered electro-rock lane — harder-edged but with the same dancefloor intelligence.

albums.title: II
albums.reason: Your love of Radiohead's emotional weight and Underworld's hypnotic pulse points straight at Moderat — this album is where those two worlds fuse at their most affecting.

albums.title: Immunity
albums.reason: With Orbital and Leftfield so deep in your all-time DNA, Immunity is a landmark you'd almost certainly connect with — it builds the same sense of euphoric release through pure electronic architecture.

albums.title: Music Has the Right to Children
albums.reason: Your all-time taste runs through the electronic and the melancholic — Boards of Canada sit at the intersection of both and this debut is one of the genre's most beloved records.

albums.title: The Last Resort
albums.reason: The atmospheric depth you love in Air and Röyksopp finds a darker, more textural counterpart in Trentemøller's debut — a cult classic that deserves your ears.

albums.title: Kill for Love
albums.reason: The melancholy and space you love in Radiohead and James Blake threads directly into Chromatics — this double album is cinematic, hypnotic and deeply underheard.

albums.title: Flag
albums.reason: Daft Punk and Justice both drew from the playful, theatrical side of electronic music — Yello were doing it first and this album is their peak, absurd and brilliant in equal measure.

albums.title: Every Day
albums.reason: The emotional intelligence you bring to James Blake and Radiohead maps beautifully onto The Cinematic Orchestra — this album is rich, unhurried and quietly devastating.

albums.title: Living Fields
albums.reason: Your 12-month arc toward Nils Frahm, Hania Rani, and Brambles points straight at Portico Quartet — this album sits at the edge of jazz and ambient with the same quiet intensity you've been drawn to.

albums.title: Eingya
albums.reason: With Brambles and Ólafur Arnalds both in your recent rotation, Helios fills a similar space — lush, textural, and warm in a way that feels less clinical than most ambient records.

albums.title: On the Go
albums.reason: Your recent deep dives into Oscar Peterson and Bill Withers signal a real pull toward jazz warmth and soul — Matthew Halsall brings that same feeling into a more contemporary, meditative spiritual jazz space.

albums.title: Elaenia
albums.reason: Your 12 months span Four Tet, Nils Frahm and Max Cooper — Floating Points' debut album sits right at the centre of that Venn diagram, flowing from jazz to electronic and back without effort.

albums.title: Persona
albums.reason: With Apparat and Martin Kohlstedt both in your recent rotation, Rival Consoles is the next step — deeply personal, melodically driven electronic music with the same emotional architecture you've been exploring.

albums.title: Gold
albums.reason: Your recent love of Bill Withers and Charles Bradley shows a pull toward music with real human presence and soul — Alabaster DePlume carries that spirit into something altogether more unexpected and moving.

albums.title: The Return
albums.reason: The Oscar Peterson Trio and Sault both feature in your recent top plays — Kamaal Williams bridges that classic jazz sensibility with a modern London groove that you'd likely find immediately compelling.

albums.title: Voyage Direct
albums.reason: Your recent plays around Tosca, DJ Koze and Super Flu point to a love of warm, deep, emotionally resonant house — Tom Trago's debut is a European cult classic in that exact vein.

albums.title: Prehension
albums.reason: Joep Beving is all over your last month and Liminal has been on repeat — Prehension is his debut and arguably his most intimate, the place where his minimalist piano style crystallised.

albums.title: Waltz for Debby
albums.reason: You've been deep in Bill Evans this month with New Jazz Conceptions — this live Village Vanguard recording is his most beloved and essential, a perfect next step from where you are.

albums.title: The Universe Smiles Upon You
albums.reason: Con Todo El Mundo has been on your recent list and Khruangbin are deep in your current rotation — their debut has even more of that dusty, hypnotic warmth and feels perfectly suited to right now.

albums.title: Geography
albums.reason: You've been playing Tom Misch's Full Circle recently and his breakout debut Geography sits in the same warm, jazz-touched groove — arguably even more essential and ripe for a full listen.

albums.title: We Get Requests
albums.reason: You've had Night Train on 36 plays in recent months — We Get Requests is the natural companion, same trio at their most melodically accessible and joyful.

albums.title: In Plain Sight
albums.reason: Return to Zero has been all over your last week — In Plain Sight is his debut and where that New Orleans-soaked boogie-soul sound first arrived, fully formed and irresistible.

albums.title: Smokers Delight
albums.reason: You've been playing In A Space Outta Dub recently and Nightmares on Wax are in your current month rotation — Smokers Delight is their lush, deep classic and a perfect complement.

albums.title: Tlapa
albums.reason: You've practically lived inside Suzuki this month — Tlapa is their more recent release and carries the same warm, organic electronic drift but feels fresher and less familiar.

songs.title: Harder, Better, Faster, Stronger
songs.reason: Discovery is one of your most-played albums by your most-played artist — this track is a vault classic that probably hasn't had a dedicated play in a while.

songs.title: All I Need
songs.reason: Reckoner is your third most-played track ever, and In Rainbows is clearly deep in your soul — All I Need is the album's most devastating slow-burner and the one that creeps up on you.

songs.title: The Wilhelm Scream
songs.reason: You have two James Blake tracks in your all-time top 20 with over 240 combined plays — The Wilhelm Scream is one of his most emotionally raw performances and a worthy vault revisit.

songs.title: Poor Leno
songs.reason: Röyksopp are your fifth most-played artist all-time — Poor Leno is one of their most iconic tracks from Melody A.M. and a reminder of why they captured you in the first place.

songs.title: Halcyon and On and On
songs.reason: Orbital sits at 777 all-time plays and this is one of their most transcendent pieces — an eternal track that belongs in any revisit of your electronic bedrock.

songs.title: Cowgirl
songs.reason: Born Slippy is in your all-time top tracks, but Cowgirl is the deeper Underworld cut that rewards the patient listener — a vault gem for your 633-play relationship with this band.

songs.title: When the Levee Breaks
songs.reason: Led Zeppelin sit at nearly 1,000 all-time plays in your library — this is one of their most elemental recordings, the kind of track that sounds different every few years of your life.

songs.title: Playground Love
songs.reason: Air are in your all-time top seven with over 1,000 plays — this Virgin Suicides cut is one of their most beautiful and melancholic moments, perfect for a quiet evening revisit.

songs.title: Bad Kingdom
songs.reason: Your all-time love of Radiohead's emotional pull and Underworld's hypnotic build leads straight here — Bad Kingdom is Moderat at their most affecting and should absolutely be on your radar.

songs.title: Open Eye Signal
songs.reason: With Orbital and Leftfield so central to your all-time taste, this track delivers the same sense of ascent and release — one of the most celebrated electronic tracks of the 2010s and something you'd love.

songs.title: Roygbiv
songs.reason: The melodic warmth you love in Röyksopp and Air finds a more abstract, textural counterpart here — a perfect entry point into one of electronic music's most beloved back catalogues.

songs.title: Moan
songs.reason: If Leftfield's Release the Pressure is a track you've played 120 times, Moan belongs in the same headspace — slow, building, and deeply compelling in a way that gets under your skin.

songs.title: To Build a Home
songs.reason: Your all-time emotional touchstones — James Blake's Postpone, Radiohead's Reckoner — point directly at this track, which carries that same sense of something precious and fragile being held up to the light.

songs.title: Shadow
songs.reason: The melancholy and restraint you love in James Blake and Radiohead is exactly what Chromatics channel — Shadow is one of their most hypnotic tracks and a genuine discovery waiting to happen.

songs.title: Pogo
songs.reason: Justice and Daft Punk are two of your most-played artists ever — Pogo sits right in that French electro-rock lane and is one of the most purely enjoyable tracks in that era's canon.

songs.title: The Rhythm Divine
songs.reason: The playful, theatrical streak running through Justice and Daft Punk has a clear ancestor in Yello — this track is gloriously strange and completely irresistible, a genuine all-time discovery for your taste.

songs.title: LesAlpx
songs.reason: Your recent arc through Four Tet, Apparat and Nils Frahm points straight at Floating Points — this track from Elaenia floats between jazz and electronic with exactly the kind of patience and beauty you've been seeking.

songs.title: Flores
songs.reason: The combination of Nils Frahm's minimalism and Oscar Peterson's jazz warmth in your recent listening creates a perfect runway for Portico Quartet — Flores is their most quietly devastating single moment.

songs.title: Badder Than Before
songs.reason: Your recent deep dives into Oscar Peterson and Bill Withers show a real hunger for jazz with genuine soul — this track from Matthew Halsall carries that same warmth into a more meditative, contemporary space.

songs.title: Untravel
songs.reason: Apparat's Hum of Maybe has been one of your most-played tracks this year — Rival Consoles' Untravel lives in the same emotionally weighted electronic space and should feel immediately right.

songs.title: Bless This Morning Year
songs.reason: Your recent rotation of Brambles and Ólafur Arnalds shows a pull toward ambient music with genuine emotional warmth — Helios does this better than almost anyone and this track is his most immediately beautiful.

songs.title: Coming With You
songs.reason: The humanity and directness you love in Bill Withers and Charles Bradley runs through Alabaster DePlume's music — this track is moving, gently funny, and feels like nothing else in your current orbit.

songs.title: Salaam
songs.reason: Your recent love of Oscar Peterson's trio playing and Sault's modern London soul points right at Kamaal Williams — Salaam is where his jazz instincts and groove sensibility hit their perfect balance.

songs.title: The Return of the Raver
songs.reason: Your recent rotation of Tosca, DJ Koze and Super Flu shows a real appreciation for warm, emotionally resonant house — this track from Tom Trago is a European cult favourite in exactly that vein.

songs.title: Maria También
songs.reason: You've had Khruangbin on heavy rotation this week and this track is one of their most irresistible — a dusty, looping groove that fits the current hazy, unhurried mood perfectly.

songs.title: Hernieuwd
songs.reason: Joep Beving has been all over your last month and Liminal has been your anchor — Hernieuwd from Prehension is where his minimalist voice feels most complete and unhurried.

songs.title: Peace Piece
songs.reason: You've been deep in Bill Evans this month and this solo piece is one of jazz piano's most hypnotic recordings — a single sustained modal meditation that aligns perfectly with your current contemplative mood.

songs.title: Alameda
songs.reason: Return to Zero has been on constant rotation this week — Alameda from In Plain Sight is where his boogie-piano soul sound feels most effortless and joyful.

songs.title: Nightrider
songs.reason: You've been playing Tom Misch recently and Geography is where his sound was at its most expansive — Nightrider is one of the album's most atmospheric, after-hours moments.

songs.title: Chocolate Elvis
songs.reason: You've practically lived inside Suzuki this month — Chocolate Elvis from their Opera album shows a different, deeper side of Tosca that fits right alongside the warm drift you've been in.

songs.title: Hymn to Freedom
songs.reason: Night Train has had 36 plays in your recent rotation — Hymn to Freedom is Peterson at his most emotionally generous, a piece with genuine weight that rewards the current reflective mood.

songs.title: Les Nuits
songs.reason: You've been playing Nightmares on Wax this month and this track from Smokers Delight is one of their most beloved — a warm, slow-motion gem that fits perfectly into the late-evening atmosphere you're currently in.

djSets.setName: HMWL Podcast 430: Niconé & David Hasert (Kompakt / Get Physical / HMWL)
Daphni - FOLD, London. Nov 22, 2025. 7hr DJ set
Klausmarie b2b Alec Troniq - RAVIN' RAVIN' 2026 @ Sektor Evolution
BENT THE KNEE TO HER VOL. 1
